summary: Gruber reflects on the passing of Brad Cox, creator of Objective-C, by highlighting tributes from developers Gus Mueller and Ken Kocienda. He explores why Objective-C is polarizing: its unusual syntax looks ugly to some programmers but feels liberating to others. Gruber draws a distinction between programmers who view source code as their product versus those who view the app as their product, arguing that Objective-C devotees fall into the latter camp. He uses an extended analogy comparing novels to screenplays — one is the product itself, the other is a means to an end. The piece celebrates Objective-C's unique ability to make developers feel like they were directly shaping their apps rather than writing instructions for a compiler.
key_points: Objective-C is polarizing because its syntax is visually distinctive and unusual compared to most modern languages, but this was a necessary consequence of layering object-oriented features on top of C
Gruber identifies two types of programmers: those who view source code as their craft and those who view the finished app as their craft, with Objective-C appealing strongly to the latter group
The novel vs. screenplay analogy illustrates how Objective-C's syntax serves a purpose — like screenplay formatting, it's brutal but functional toward creating the real product
Ken Kocienda's tribute frames Objective-C not as a language but as a medium for thinking and creation, like chisels and marble for a sculptor
Objective-C developers describe the language as giving them direct access to shaping the app itself, not just writing instructions
money_quotes: There are some good programmers who view their source code as their product. The source code they write is, in their minds, the thing they are crafting.
There are other good programmers who view the app they are creating as their product. The app is the thing they are creating and the source code is just a part of the process.
It's the difference between crafting code and crafting apps.
A novel is the product — the prose is meant to be enjoyed and should, in and of itself, be beautiful. A screenplay is just a means toward an end: making a movie.

I'd rather read a Quentin Tarantino screenplay than most novels. Yet I'd also always rather watch a Quentin Tarantino movie than read his screenplay for it — because that's the thing he's working toward.
They speak about it like Kocienda does, like it gave them the ability to put their fingers on the apps themselves.
Not writing a recipe for baking a cake, but somehow baking a cake directly, and tweaking it to taste better and look prettier as it's cooking.
key_insight: The best programming languages aren't necessarily the ones that produce the prettiest source code, but the ones that make developers feel like they're directly shaping the product itself.
